era_context: Old Kingdom Egypt in terminal decline; Akkadian Empire at zenith under Naram-Sin, though internal strains are mounting; Indus Valley Civilization in mature phase; Early Bronze Age III in the Levant and Anatolia. This year marks a symbolic boundary in some chronological systems, though no specific global event marks it.

events.title: Egyptian Old Kingdom: Sixth Dynasty in final decline or recently ended; First Intermediate Period begins
events.description: The Sixth Dynasty either ends around this time or has recently ended. The First Intermediate Period (c. 2181–2055 BCE) begins, characterized by political fragmentation, the rise of regional dynasties, and the loss of centralized pharaonic authority. Upper Egypt is divided among competing nomarchs. Lower Egypt may be controlled by rival factions. The transition is marked by the absence of monumental building and the proliferation of local administrative records. Some scholars place the dynasty's end earlier (c. 2184 BCE); others place it later (c. 2160 BCE).
key_figures: Pepi II (if still reigning) or his successor
Nomarchs of Upper Egypt (Ankhtifi, Siese, Kheti)
events.certainty_note: The exact year of the Sixth Dynasty's end is uncertain, with scholarly estimates ranging from c. 2184 to 2160 BCE. The year -2350 is assigned to this period by convention, but the specific events of this year are not documented.

events.title: Akkadian Empire: continued military campaigns; Gutian pressure increases
events.description: Naram-Sin continues his reign, conducting military campaigns to suppress rebellions and maintain control over distant provinces. The Gutians, a highland people from the Zagros, pose an increasing threat to Akkadian control. Rebellions in conquered territories become more frequent. The empire remains at its territorial maximum, but internal strains and external pressures are accelerating. The empire's collapse will occur within the next few decades.
events.certainty_note: Naram-Sin's reign is well-documented, but specific year-to-year events are not always precisely dated. His reign is generally dated c. 2254–2218 BCE.

events.title: Indus Valley Civilization: mature phase continues; standardized urban and economic systems
events.description: The mature Harappan civilization continues to flourish. Major urban centers such as Mohenjo-daro, Harappa, Dholavira, and Kalibangan maintain their standardized planning, infrastructure, and craft production. Trade networks extend to Mesopotamia, the Persian Gulf, and Central Asia. The civilization is characterized by remarkable stability and continuity, with no evidence of major political upheaval, warfare, or environmental crisis during this period. The civilization will remain stable for several more centuries before beginning its decline around 1900 BCE.

sources.name: Archaeological excavations at Mohenjo-daro, Harappa, and other Indus sites

events.certainty_note: The mature Harappan phase is well-established archaeologically. The year -2350 falls securely within this period based on radiocarbon dating and stratigraphic evidence.

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of the Sixth Dynasty's end is disputed. Some scholars place it around -2184 BCE, others around -2160 BCE. The year -2350 is assigned to this period by convention, but it may not correspond to any specific event. The causes of the Old Kingdom collapse are debated: climate change (reduced Nile inundation) is often cited, but some scholars emphasize internal administrative fragmentation and the rise of provincial power as primary factors. The Akkadian Empire's collapse is traditionally attributed to Gutian invasion, but some scholars argue for internal rebellion and administrative breakdown as primary causes.
historiographic_note: The year -2350 is essentially undocumented in specific terms. The entries describe the general historical context and trends. Ancient chronologies for the third millennium BCE are uncertain to within ±5–10 years, and year-to-year documentation is rare. The historical record for this year is essentially identical to -2354, -2353, -2352, and -2351. The year -2350 is sometimes used as a symbolic boundary in chronological systems (e.g., marking the transition from the Old Kingdom to the First Intermediate Period in Egypt), but no specific global event marks it. The surviving sources are heavily skewed toward Egypt and Mesopotamia; the Indus Valley Civilization is known primarily through archaeology, and much of the world (Sub-Saharan Africa, northern Europe, East Asia, Oceania) is invisible in surviving records.

graph_edges.note: End of Sixth Dynasty leads to First Intermediate Period
Mature Harappan phase precedes civilization's decline
